coredata.dc:title: N-doped and Cu,N-doped carbon dots as corrosion inhibitor for mild steel corrosion in acid medium
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Padhan, Subash
Rout, Tapan Kumar
Nair, Udayabhanu G.
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and Cu,N-doped carbon dots (Cu,N-CDs) with various Cu doping amounts were explored as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel substrate. Material characterizations of synthesized materials were performed using UV-Vis, FTIR and XRD, TEM and XPS techniques. Electrochemical corrosion testing showed enhanced corrosion inhibition performance for Cu,N-CDs relative to N-CDs. The inhibition efficiencies were directly proportional to concentrations of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs, with more than 97% achieved at 100 ppm of Cu,N-CDs. Gravimetric measurements at temperatures ranging from 40 to 70 ℃ revealed a slight increase in inhibition efficiencies from 99.5 % to 99.7 % for higher Cu doped Cu,N-CDs. Protective nature of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs was accredited to physicochemical adsorption and inhibitor film formation. In addition, the growth of an electroless like Cu deposit on the mild steel surface contributed to better inhibition performances of Cu,N-CDs. The inhibition mechanism was supported by surface characterization of inhibited steel substrate.

head.ce:title: N-doped and Cu,N-doped carbon dots as corrosion inhibitor for mild steel corrosion in acid medium

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and Cu,N-doped carbon dots (Cu,N-CDs) with various Cu doping amounts were explored as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel substrate. Material characterizations of synthesized materials were performed using UV-Vis, FTIR and XRD, TEM and XPS techniques. Electrochemical corrosion testing showed enhanced corrosion inhibition performance for Cu,N-CDs relative to N-CDs. The inhibition efficiencies were directly proportional to concentrations of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs, with more than 97% achieved at 100 ppm of Cu,N-CDs. Gravimetric measurements at temperatures ranging from 40 to 70 ℃ revealed a slight increase in inhibition efficiencies from 99.5 % to 99.7 % for higher Cu doped Cu,N-CDs. Protective nature of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs was accredited to physicochemical adsorption and inhibitor film formation. In addition, the growth of an electroless like Cu deposit on the mild steel surface contributed to better inhibition performances of Cu,N-CDs. The inhibition mechanism was supported by surface characterization of inhibited steel substrate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical Abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel, a class of low carbon steel, has been used extensively in daily life and various industries such as oil and gas, construction, and power plants owing to its low cost and superior mechanical properties [1,2]. However, mild steel tends to corrode severely in aggressive acid environments, which becomes a cause of concern as acids are extensively used for cleaning, pickling, descaling and oil well acidizing. For example, the petroleum industry uses explicitly 15% HCl acid solution to dissolve calcareous materials such as limestone, dolomites and carbonates from reservoir rocks to increase the oil flow. But due to the corrosive action of HCl, the steel pipes suffer heavy corrosion and consequences of this can be increase in production cost, material failure, oil spillage and environmental health hazards [3,4]. Therefore, the use of corrosion inhibitors in acid solution suits best to reduce the severe damaging effect of corrosion on steel substrates. Corrosion inhibitors primarily form a passive layer or barrier film on the metal surface by adsorption, hindering the active anodic dissolution of metals and cathodic reactions such as hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions [5].

Inorganic compounds such as chromates, dichromates, phosphates, tungstate, nitrates etc., were explored previously. These compounds form a strong passive layer in harsh corrosive environments to reduce corrosion [6,7]. However, owing to acute toxicity and potential health hazards, use of some inorganic corrosion inhibitors have been limited [8]. Organic compounds containing aromatic rings, conjugated π electrons and multiple functional groups with electronegative heteroatoms such as N, P, S and O have been studied with satisfactory corrosion inhibition performance [9]. These organic inhibitors principally adsorb onto the metal surface by physical and chemical means based on electrostatic and donor-acceptor interaction. The adsorbed inhibitor molecules then form a protective layer, reducing corrosion on the metal surface. Nevertheless, many organic inhibitors still possess toxicity, long synthetic route and high cost of synthesis [10]. Therefore, attention must be shifted towards eco-friendly, high water solubility, low cost and easy synthesis methods to develop new corrosion inhibitor materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered tremendous focus because of their peculiar characteristics such as high fluorescence, water-solubility, low or no toxicity, and ease of functionalization. Thus, carbon dots have been examined across fields of biosensing, drug delivery, photocatalysis and light emitting diode [11–17]. In recent times carbon dots are being explored in the area of corrosion protection. Cui and coworkers reported nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) prepared from o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine precursors for carbon steel corrosion and accomplished substantial corrosion inhibition performance in the presence of CDs [18]. Qiang et al. studied 4-aminosalicylic acid derived N-CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper corrosion protection in H2SO4 [19]. The synthesized N-CDs were established to form stable inhibitor film with a physicochemical adsorption mechanism. Imidazole functionalized CDs investigated by Yang and coworkers demonstrated increasing corrosion inhibition efficiency with increasing concentration and immersion time for carbon steel substrate [20]. Cen et al. developed N and S co-doped CDs, which reduced corrosion of carbon steel in NaCl medium by creating a hydrophobic barrier film [21]. Several other research on carbon steel corrosion inhibition by heteroatom-doped CDs with improved anticorrosion behaviour can be found in the literature [22–25]. Literature on heteroatom-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors suggests enhanced corrosion inhibition performance, following the development of robust inhibitor film by physical or chemical adsorption of inhibitors. Though heteroatom doped CDs perform well in restricting corrosion, all existing reports were primarily focused on this.

Metal ion and heteroatom doped CDs such as Mg,N-CDs, Cu,N-CDs and Zn,N-CDs, etc., found to show exceptional properties like bioimaging, antibacterial activity, selective sensing of hazardous materials and bactericidal activity [26–29]. However, their application in corrosion inhibition has not been investigated thus far. Metal ion and heteroatom-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors may adsorb on steel substrate via heteroatoms and reduce corrosion. Simultaneously, metal ions in CDs may build up a metal ion-inhibitor complex film or passive metal oxide film, enhancing corrosion inhibition properties. Cu being a noble metal, its doping effect on corrosion phenomena could be interesting. With this aim, we have synthesized N-CDs and Cu and nitrogen-doped CDs (Cu,N-CDs) and studied the possible role of doped Cu ion in corrosion inhibition process.

The present study investigated metal ion and heteroatom doped carbon dots for corrosion inhibition of mild steel to explore a new dimension. In the present study, Cu and N doped carbon dots (Cu,N-CDs) were synthesized with varying Cu doping concentrations by hydrothermal method. First, N-CDs were synthesized with an optimized concentration of nitrogen precursor (ethylenediamine). Then it was modified with Cu source (cupric chloride) to obtain Cu,N-CDs. The synthesized materials were characterized by UV-Vis, FTIR, TEM, and XPS techniques. The corrosion protection performances and inhibition mechanism of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs were thoroughly discussed by electrochemical analysis, gravimetric method, surface morphology and topography, XPS and XRD.

Citric acid monohydrate (Lobachemie, India), cupric chloride dihydrate (Alfa Aser), Ethylenediamine (EDA) and Hydrochloric acid (Merck, India) were used as received. Commercial mild steel sheet was obtained from the market in India and used for corrosion studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped (N-CDs) and Cu,N-doped (Cu,N-CDs) carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs were synthesized following an established method with slight modification [30]. In detail, citric acid (1 g) as carbon precursor was mixed with three different concentrations of EDA (285, 320 and 385 µl) in 10 ml of deionized water to obtain clear homogenous solutions. Then the obtained solutions were hydrothermally treated to a fixed temperature of 200 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling, dark reddish-brown colour solutions were achieved. It was then filtered, centrifuged, followed by dialysis and hot air drying to get carbon dots powder. For reference, citric acid carbon dots (CA-CDs) without nitrogen doping were also prepared similarly. Electrochemical methods of corrosion analysis were utilized to optimize EDA concentrations in N-CDs, which revealed 320 µl as the optimized concentration of EDA in N-CDs. Here, only N-CDs doped with optimized concentrations of EDA are discussed. Hence, hereafter, N-CDs will denote CDs doped with 320 µl of EDA. Results of N-CDs-2 (CDs doped with 285 µl of EDA), N-CDs-3 (CDs doped with 385 µl of EDA) and CA-CDs can be found in supplementary information.

Cu,N-CDs were synthesized following the above preparation procedure with cupric chloride as copper source. Briefly, 1 g citric acid, 320 µl ethylenediamine and various amounts of cupric chloride (0.150, 0.40 and 0.55 g) were mixed in 10 ml of deionized water followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 ℃ for 6 h. The obtained reddish-brown solutions were subjected to a similar purification process to get carbon dots powder. Cu,N-CDs with increasing Cu doping amounts were designated as Cu,N-CDs-1, Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3. A schematic representation of Cu,N-CDs synthesis is illustrated in Fig. 1.

Mild steel specimen of 10 mm × 10 mm × 0.2 mm was used as the metal substrate for corrosion studies. The surface of the mild steel sample was embedded in epoxy resin for electrochemical study. The surface preparation was done by polishing the substrate with sand paper (from 180 to 2000 grade) and then degreased with distilled water and acetone before use. 15% HCl was used as corrosive electrolyte to study the corrosion behaviour of mild steel in absence (blank) and presence of inhibitor. Inhibitor solutions were prepared by adding various inhibitor concentrations to 15 % HCl solution.

UV-Visible spectra of synthesized material were recorded in water with a Shimadzu UV-1800 UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra of doped CDs were measured in water as solvent with the help of Perkin Elmer (Model: LS55) fluorescence spectrometer. FTIR spectra were used to analyze functionalities of doped carbon dots. FTIR measurements were carried out in KBR-pellet mode using Perkin Elmer FTIR spectrum in the frequency range of 4000–500 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs were accomplished with the help of an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Smartlab, SmartLab Studio II) with a scan speed of 5 degrees per minute, step size of 0.02 degrees and Copper Kα1 X-ray source at a wavelength of 1.54 Å. HRTEM (High resolution transmission electron microscopy) analysis of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs was examined by Thermo Scientific (Model: Talos F200X G2l). XPS spectra of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs were investigated by PHI 5000 versa probe III. Elemental analysis of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs were also performed by EDS (energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) attached with a FESEM instrument (Model: FESEM Supra55, Carl Zeiss, Germany). For EDS elemental analysis, powdered N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs samples were prepared by spreading over a carbon tape.

Electrochemical analysis of mild steel in presence and absence of inhibitor was carried out in a CHI 660 C electrochemical workstation with three electrode cell system. Mild steel samples (with 1 cm2 exposed area) was used as working electrode and saturated calomel and platinum as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The corrosion properties of blank and inhibitor samples were evaluated in 15% aqueous HCl solution. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data were recorded at open circuit potential (OCP) in the frequency range of 100 KHz to 0.01 Hz with 5 mV sinusoidal perturbation. The raw EIS data were fitted with EC lab software (Version-11.36). From the fitted data, various impedance parameters were extracted and analyzed. Potentiodynamic polarization study was carried out in the range of ± 250 mV from OCP at a scan rate of 1 mV/s.
E
corr
and
i
corr
values were obtained from Tafel extrapolation for blank and inhibited steel substrates.

Gravimetric measurements of mild steel samples in presence and absence of inhibitors were conducted following ASTM standard procedure at temperatures 40, 50, 60, and 70 ℃ by immersing in 15% HCl solution for 6 h. Immersed steel samples were cleaned, dried and weighed accurately before and after. Weight loss of steel samples was used for corrosion rate (CR) and inhibition efficiency
η
calculation with the following equations.

denote corrosion rates in absence and presence of inhibitor.

The polished mild steel substrates were dipped in 15% HCl solution for 6 h in absence and presence of inhibitor, washed with distilled water and dried before analysis. Surface morphology and elemental analysis of blank and inhibitor samples were analyzed by FESEM-EDS. Inhibitor adsorption and bonding on steel surface were evaluated by XPS. Surface roughness of inhibited and uninhibited steel surface were investigated by AFM. XRD of mild steel surface in absence and presence of inhibitors were also examined.

Fig. 2(a) represents the UV-Visible absorption spectra of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs in water. The π-π * transition of aromatic CC in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs appeared at around 238 nm while peak at 338 nm was assigned to n-π * transition of CO and CN moieties. The inset of Fig. 2(a) shows the intense blue colour fluorescence of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs on exposure to UV light of 365 nm wavelength [27]. The excitation dependent fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs in the range of 300–380 nm were depicted in Fig. S1 (SI). An apparent increase in fluorescence intensity from excitation wavelength 300–340 nm and a decrease afterward was witnessed. N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs showed an optimal fluorescence emission intensity at around 430 nm at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The effect of pH and temperatures on the fluorescence intensity of synthesized doped CDs was also studied at a fixed excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The effect of pH on fluorescence intensity is demonstrated in Fig. S2. It was observed that very low or high pH has a detrimental impact on the fluorescence intensity of various synthesized CDs. The lowest fluorescence intensity was recorded at a pH of 0.4, whilst the highest fluorescence intensity was observed at neutral pH. The temperature variations, too, have an impact on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. As can be seen from Fig. S3, with increasing temperature, the fluorescence intensity was found to decrease with the lowest fluorescence intensity observed at 60 ℃ temperature.

FTIR spectra were recorded (Fig. 2(b)) to understand the surface structure and functionalities in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs. The broad peaks at around 3400 and 3200 cm−1 were ascribed to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. The absorption bands at 2914 cm−1, 1665 cm−1 and 1555 cm−1 were accredited to C-H stretching, CO stretching and aromatic CC stretching. The aromatic heterocyclic and aliphatic C-N-C stretching and C-O stretching were observed at 1383 cm−1, 1134 cm−1 and 1052 cm−1, respectively. An additional peak at 940 cm−1 in the metal-sensitive region (920–100 cm−1) was ascribed to N-Cu-N band in Cu,N-CDs. The presence of nitrogen and carboxylic functional groups could have facilitated Cu chelation in Cu,N-CDs [32,33]. Thus, the results from FTIR suggested successful doping in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs with abundant surface functional groups, which accounted for their high water solubility.

High resolution TEM images of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs are presented in Fig. 3(a) and (b). Spherical dots structured morphology with average sizes around 10 nm were observed for N-CDs, while larger average particle sizes of around 18 nm were noticed for Cu,N-CDs. The crystalline nature of Cu,N-CDs was noticeable in high resolution image with a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm, associated with (002) lattice plane of graphite [34]. However, no lattice fringes could be found in N-CDs due to their amorphous nature.

The XRD pattern of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs is represented in Fig. 3(c). An intense single broad peak at 23º was observed for N-CDs which can be assigned to graphitic carbon, indicating the core structure of carbon dots as graphitic. The peak may also be attributed to the disordered carbon [34]. A broad graphitic carbon peak for Cu,N-CDs-1 was observed at 25.6º with d-spacing value of 0.34 nm, while for Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3, sharp peaks appeared at 26.3º. Additionally, with increasing concentration of copper doping in Cu,N-CDs, some extra peaks due to Cu were observed for Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3, which further confirms the successful doping of Cu. A peak centered at 2θ, 29.5º in Cu,N-CD-2 and Cu,N-CD-3 can be assigned Cu2O. Cu,N-CD-2 and Cu,N-CD-3 showed several CuO peaks at around 32.6º, 34º, 38.4º, 48.3º, 57.8º and metallic Cu peak at 43.3º [35,36].

Elemental analysis of doped carbon dots was accessed by EDS spectra. The corresponding spectral images were demonstrated in Fig. S4 (SI). The presence of doping elements in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs established successful synthesis of doped carbon dots. Details of elemental composition in weight percentage were listed in Table S1.

To further know about the surface composition and oxidation states of elements present, XPS spectroscopy was performed for N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs. Here, XPS spectra of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-2 (optimized Cu concentration) are illustrated. XPS spectra of Cu,N-CDs-1 and Cu,N-CDs-3 can be found in SI Fig S5. As shown in Fig. 4(a), survey spectra established the presence of C, N, O in N-CDs and C, N, O and Cu in Cu,N-CDs-2, indicating successful doping of N and Cu. The high resolution spectra of C, N, O in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-2 were depicted in Fig. 4(b)-(g). C 1 s spectra could be deconvoluted into three peaks C-C/CC, C-O/C-N and CO at 284.6, 285.6, 287.8 eV for N-CDs and 284.6, 285.3, 286.5 eV for Cu,N-CDs-2, respectively [37]. Pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen were assigned to deconvoluted N 1 s peaks at 398.6, 400.1 and 400.8 eV for N-CDs and 398.2, 399.2 and 400.2 eV for Cu,N-CDs, respectively [32]. On the other hand, two peaks at 531.2 and 532.7 eV could be found for O 1 s spectra representing C-O and CO [38]. These observations further supported the various functional groups detected in FTIR analysis of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs.

Cu 2p elemental scan in Cu,N-CDs-2 (Fig. 4(h)) displayed spin-orbit coupling (∆E = 19.7 eV) with two predominant peaks for Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 at 934 and 954 eV, respectively assigned to Cu(II) ion. Moreover, two strong satellite peaks at 942.3 (doublet at 941.3 and 943.9 eV) and 962.3 eV were characteristic of Cu (II) ions, revealing the bulk of Cu in Cu,N-CDs exists in divalent cationic form. However, the deconvolution of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 also resulted in two small peaks at 931.9 and 952 eV, respectively, which could be explained by the presence of Cu(0) and Cu(I) species at low concentrations [36]. Similar results were found in the XPS spectra of Cu,N-CDs-1 and Cu,N-CDs-3. The observed oxidation states of Cu are consistent with XRD analysis, which revealed CuO, Cu2O, and metallic Cu peaks. The chelation of doped Cu+2 ion could be possible through nitrogen centres of Cu,N-CDs with N-Cu-N bonding, supported by FTIR spectra.

To optimize the EDA concentration in N-doped CDs on the basis of corrosion inhibition properties, EIS and polarization test were conducted. N-CDs (doped with 320 µl of EDA) was found to provide superior corrosion resistance against mild steel corrosion in 15% HCl. EIS and polarization data of N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and CA-CDs can be found in the supplementary information. Here, electrochemical anticorrosion performance of optimized N-CDs was evaluated and discussed along with various amounts of Cu doped Cu,N-CDs. After 1 h immersion in HCl, EIS spectra of mild steel samples were obtained in the presence and absence of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs. Fig. 5 displays the Nyquist plot for mild steel in presence and absence of different concentrations of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs solution. A single depressed capacitive loop at a high frequency region ascribed to metal-electrolyte charge transfer reaction can be witnessed for all inhibitor samples and blank steel. The depressed nature of capacitive arc may be attributed to the surface inhomogeneity, roughness or frequency dispersion on the metal substrate [39]. In the entire studied concentrations range (25–500 ppm) of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs, the diameter of arc was higher than the blank steel, which indicated the suppression of corrosion process on steel surface owing to adsorption of CDs. Furthermore, increasing CDs inhibitor concentration resulted in a significant rise in capacitive arc diameter. This concentration dependent enhancement in arc diameter could be due to adsorption of more inhibitor molecules followed by successive film formation with improved film denseness on the metal substrate, thereby blocking the interaction of corrosive ions with the metal [40].

Bode impedance modulus plots at different concentrations of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs were depicted in Fig. 6. The elevation of impedance modulus, |Z| value at frequency 0.01 Hz could be evidenced with rising concentrations of inhibitors and the highest value was recorded at 500 ppm for all inhibitors. The |Z| 0.01 Hz values for mild steel in the absence of inhibitor was found to be 16 Ω cm2. However, in presence of N-CDs, CuN-CDs-1, CuN-CDs-2 and CuN-CDs-3, the values raised to 531, 655, 740 and 694 Ω cm2, respectively. These results pointed out enhancement in corrosion inhibition with the addition of inhibitor. Moreover, Cu,N-CDs demonstrated better corrosion inhibition with higher values of |Z| 0.01 Hz than N-CDs indicating the significant role of Cu doping in corrosion inhibition mechanism. On the other hand, between different amounts of Cu doped Cu,N-CDs sample, Cu,N-CDs-2 outperformed others.

The Bode phase angle plot ( Fig. 7) revealed increasing maximum phase angle with addition of CDs inhibitor. The increasing phase angle suggested the shift towards higher capacitive behaviour and decreased corrosion process. It may also be noted that Cu,N-CDs samples offered significantly higher phase angle compared to N-CDs and approached towards pure capacitive nature. In addition, a single time constant was observed for all inhibitors relating to the charge transfer process and a dwindling tail in some cases owing to the relaxation process. An inductive behaviour at starting few high frequencies that is phase angle below zero degree was noticed, which may be accredited to wire resistance due to longer cable connection between the working electrode and potentiostat [41]. Though a small inductance may also arise due to instrumental artifacts, it was ruled out as the same result was witnessed after testing in couples of other potentiostats.

To better understand the various characteristics of the EIS, the Nyquist plot was fitted with the equivalent circuit model given in Fig. 8. The notations, L,

The inhibition efficiency

signify the polarization resistance in the presence and absence of inhibitor. Here, polarization resistance is defined as the sum of charge transfer resistance and the film resistance in presence of inhibitor. The fitted values are listed in Table 1. From Table 1, it can be understood that the polarization resistance at all concentrations of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs is significantly higher than the blank steel. A gradual increase in
R
p
and η with inhibitor concentration were also noted. The highest values η of all inhibitors were attained at 500 ppm concentration. The increasing concentrations could enhance the inhibitor adsorption on the steel surface, subsequently providing better inhibition. Moreover, greater
R
p
and η for Cu,N-CDs as compared to N-CDs, established superior corrosion protection ability of Cu,N-CDs. In the case of Cu,N-CDs, with an increase in Cu doping concentration from Cu,N-CDs-1 to Cu,N-CDs-2, the inhibition efficiency improved, however with a further increase in doping concentration in Cu,N-CDs-3, the inhibition efficiency declined slightly. The variation of
R
p
with various concentrations of synthesized inhibitors are depicted in Fig. S10. Thus the corrosion inhibition performance for Cu,N-CDs followed the order Cu,N-CDs-2 ˃ Cu,N-CDs-3 ˃ Cu,N-CDs-1. Meanwhile, the smaller values of
C
dl
in presence of inhibitors compared to blank steel may be attributed to the replacement of water molecules by inhibitors with lower dielectric constant and a thicker inhibitor layer formation [43].

The improved corrosion inhibition properties of Cu,N-CDs samples compared to N-CDs may be ascribed to the role of copper doping in inhibition mechanism, which was confirmed by visual identification of mild steel surface before and after immersion in inhibitor solution. After immersion in Cu,N-CDs inhibitor solution, the shiny mild steel surface turned to a bright copper colour surface, as shown in Fig. 9, indicating the probable formation of a metallic copper film. Besides, increasingly uniform and dense copper colour film was noticeable in higher inhibitor concentration and Cu doping concentration of Cu,N-CDs. However, mild steel surface did not show any visible colour changes before and after immersion in N-CDs inhibitor solution. Thus, it may be concluded that the development of reddish brown copper colour film on steel surface was nothing but metallic Cu formed by reduction of Cu+2 from Cu (II) coordinated Cu,N-CDs to Cu(0).

The deposition was catalyzed by the difference in standard reduction potential between Cu (+0.34 V) and Fe (−0.441 V). The deposition mechanism of Cu may be accounted as electroless plating or immersion plating, similar to the example of an iron substrate immersion in a CuCl2 or CuSO4 aqueous solution. However, since immersion in Cu,N-CDs solution was characterized by adsorption of Cu,N-CDs and subsequent formation of inhibitor film on steel surface, unwanted rapid oxidation Fe due to Cu+2 were restricted. Moreover, the adhesion of Cu film on the steel surface was considerably good, possibly owing to the impact inhibitor film. Thus, it may be inferred that Cu,N-CDs inhibitor allows the formation of an extra Cu barrier layer, in addition to an adsorbed inhibitor film. As a result of this corrosion inhibition performance of Cu,N-CDs was comparatively higher than N-CDs. Except, due to the less growth of copper layer in Cu,N-CDs-1, the inhibition properties were not significantly higher than N-CDs.

Polarization curves for mild steel in the presence of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs at different concentrations along with blank steel were presented in Fig. 10. It can be observed that there is a marked decrease in cathodic and anodic current density for inhibitor samples with respect to blank, with the decline being significant for cathodic current. Further, polarization curves shifted towards cathodic direction, mainly for Cu,N-CDs inhibitor compared to blank while the small shift towards both sides observed in case of N-CDs. This cathodic curve shifting followed by cathodic current reduction indicated cathodic reactions were suppressed probably due to the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the mild steel surface. Further, at different concentrations of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs, the anodic branch of polarization curves showed a clear leap roughly after 350 mV, revealing the considerable effect of inhibitors on anodic reactions. These observations suggest a mixed inhibition mechanism with both anodic and cathodic corrosion protection for N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs. The Tafel parameters such as corrosion current
(

)
, corrosion potential (Ecorr), cathodic Tafel slope (βc), anodic Tafel slope (βa), degree of surface coverage (θ) and inhibition efficiency
η
extracted by Tafel extrapolation method for CDs inhibitor sample were presented in Table 2. Here, θ and
η %
can be calculated by the equations given below:

Where

denote corrosion current densities of blank and inhibitor samples, respectively. As illustrated in Table 2, the corrosion current values in the presence of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs, decreased significantly than in absence. As the concentration of CDs inhibitors increases, the current density decreases gradually, with the lowest values obtained at 500 ppm. Thus, inhibition efficiency followed ascending trends with increasing N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs concentrations. Cu,N-CDs offered lower corrosion current and better corrosion inhibition efficiency, supporting EIS analysis.

Moreover, between various copper doped Cu,N-CDs, Cu,N-CD-2 demonstrated the best corrosion protection performance with the lowest corrosion current and highest inhibition efficiency of 27.29 µA cm−2 and 98.5 %, respectively. Meanwhile, the Ecorr values of Cu,N-CDs displayed a negative shift towards cathodic region compared to blank steel. The maximum changes in Ecorr values of all the inhibitor molecules were between ± 85 mV with respect to the corrosion potential of blank, revealing that inhibitors are mixed type that is inhibitors curtailed both cathodic reactions and anodic metal dissolution [46]. However, a significant decrease in cathodic current and Ecorr shift in Cu,N-CDs suggested a predominant cathodic corrosion inhibition mechanism [47]. The principal cathodic inhibition mechanism in Cu,N-CDs can be explained by the preferential reduction of Cu+2 ions over H+ ions owing to their standard reduction potentials values. Since Cu is less electropositive than H+ ions in electrochemical series, the electrons released by Fe oxidation will be consumed by Cu ions in Cu,N-CDs rather than H+ ions. Thus, hydrogen evolution, a primary cathodic corrosion reaction, could be prevented due to doped Cu ions in Cu,N-CDs resulting in reduced corrosion current. A comparative literature study of carbon dots in carbon steel corrosion inhibition is presented in Table S4, including only the reported highest inhibition efficiency.

From both EIS and polarization study, it was realized that Cu,N-CDs-2 with an optimum inhibitor concentration of 500 ppm provided the highest corrosion inhibition and hence studied for longer immersion time along with N-CDs. The Nyquist plots for the optimum concentration of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-2 are displayed in Fig. 11 (a) and (b). Both inhibitor samples provided similar nature of arc at various immersion time with a depressed semicircle. The diameter of the semicircles decreased with increasing immersion time, implying decreasing charge transfer resistance and lesser corrosion protection. The Bode impedance plot (Fig. 11 (c) and (d)) also illustrated similar properties with descending order of |Z| at 0.01 Hz frequency. The phase angle plot (Fig. 11 (e) and (f)) revealed a single time constant at various immersion time. Further, a reduction in maximum phase angle was evident as immersion time increased, indicating diminished corrosion inhibition. The reducing protection performance of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-2 with the progress of time may be ascribed to desorption of inhibitor molecule as well porous inhibitor film allowing the corrosive ions to interact with steel surface [48]. Besides, declining corrosion inhibition of Cu,N-CDs-2 may also be accounted to slow deposition of Cu on steel surface, which also promoted anodic Fe oxidation.

The fitted data of Nyquist plot are presented in Table3, with the same equivalent circuit diagram given in Fig. 8. The polarization resistance of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-2 showed a descending trend with immersion time, implying decreased inhibition behaviour. Nevertheless, high
R
p
of 385.8 and 279.0 Ω.cm2 were attained for Cu,N-CDs-2 and N-CDs respectively, after 18 h of immersion, revealing slow corrosion of mild steel after prolonged immersion. The variation
R
p
with immersion time is depicted in Fig. S11.

Polarization curves at 500 ppm concentration of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-2 at different immersion time were illustrated in Fig. 12. N-CDs exhibited an increased cathodic and anodic current density with immersion time, suggesting diminishing corrosion inhibition for N-CDs with time. The cathodic curves in Cu,N-CDs-2 did not vary with immersion time. However, the anodic current was marked with an increase suggesting predominant cathodic corrosion control behaviour of Cu,N-CDs-2. Further, the results of Tafel extrapolation were summarized in Table 4. The tabulated value of
i
corr
was found to increase with immersion time for both N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-2 signifying increasing corrosion rate.

The adsorption mechanism of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs on the surface of mild steel was analyzed by adsorption isotherm. In general, the adsorption of an inhibitor molecule to a metal surface is described by electrostatic interaction or chemical bonding, referred to as physisorption and chemisorption, respectively. Adsorption isotherm models were examined from the polarization data of inhibitors and the best fit was achieved for Langmuir isotherm model using the Eq. (8) [22].

The influence of temperatures on mild steel corrosion in HCl in the presence and absence of inhibitor was studied by gravimetric method. The corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency at optimum inhibitor concentration (500 ppm) of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs inhibitors derived at 40, 50, 60 and 70 ℃ temperatures were presented in Table 5 and Fig. 14 (a), (b). Corrosion rate increased with temperature in blank and inhibited samples, though the increase was insignificant in inhibited samples. The η decreased noticeably in N-CDs with the temperature rise, while the decline was less evident in Cu,N-CDs-1. On the other hand, Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3 witnessed a minor increase in η from 40 to 60 ℃ and followed an insignificant drop. Nevertheless, inhibition efficiencies above 99.5% were achieved for Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3 at all studied temperatures, suggesting exceptional protection in the active dissolution of mild steel.

The reducing inhibition efficiency of N-CDs with rising temperature may be attributed to desorption of N-CDs from mild steel surface and faster rate of metal dissolution. However, an extra Cu barrier film in Cu-N-CDs could prevent the inhibitor desorption and corrosion reaction. Moreover, the growth of the Cu layer was much faster at higher temperatures due to the rapid anodic oxidation of Fe. Once a homogenous Cu film is developed, the film acts as a barrier layer against the ingress of corrosive ions and slows down corrosion of the metal surface. Besides, Cu growth could simultaneously stop hydrogen evolution reaction substantially. Thus, the slight increase in inhibition efficiency with temperature in Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3 may be attributed to more homogenous and dense Cu film in less time. The declining inhibition efficiency with temperature for Cu,N-CDs-1 was due to moderate growth of copper layer owing to lower Cu doping concentration.

The temperature dependence of corrosion is explained by the Arrhenius equation given by Eq. (10), where A signifies the pre-exponential factor,
E

Fig. 14 (c) and (d) depicted a good linear correlation with a linear regression coefficient nearly equal to 1. Table 6 lists the values of activation parameters at various temperatures. The Ea increased in N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs-1 inhibitors and decreased in Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3 compared to blank steel. Larger values of
E
a
are often described as elevation in activation energy barrier of metal dissolution producing lower corrosion rate. According to Radovici [52], inhibitors with decreasing inhibition efficiencies with temperature were marked by increased activation energy [53]. Contrarily, inhibitors whose inhibition efficiencies increased with temperature were characterized by lowering activation energy and reasoned it to a specific type of adsorption of inhibitors. Authors have also linked the higher values of
E
a
in the presence of inhibitors than in absence, to physical adsorption of inhibitor molecules; conversely, chemisorption phenomena were characterized by lower activation energy [54,55]. However, in this study, all CDs inhibitors had both physical and chemical interaction with the steel surface, as discussed in the adsorption isotherm section. Thus, the higher
E
a
in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-1 was accounted to more desorption of physically adsorbed inhibitors with rising temperature resulting in lowering inhibition efficiency with temperature. On the other hand, lesser value of
E
a
in Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3 was attributed to the formation of protective Cu film marked by increased inhibition efficiency with rising temperature. The protective Cu film could also prohibit desorption of physically adsorbed inhibitors to some extent and improve corrosion resistance.

The positive value of
∆
‡

0
indicated the corrosion of mild steel was endothermic in nature. N-CDs and Cu-N-CDs-1 evidenced higher values of
∆
‡
S
0
than the blank steel indicating increasing disorderness. Entropy in an inhibitor adsorption process is defined by the combination of entropy at the metal interface and solution entropy. In the course of the inhibitor adsorption process, the entropy at the metal interface decreases and solution entropy increases [56]. Thus, the higher entropy in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-1 may be accounted to adsorption of inhibitors with a higher solution entropy. Differently, more negative or lower values of
∆
‡
S
0
was obtained for Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3 suggesting more orderliness at the metal interface, which may be associated with a homogeneous Cu layer.

Surface morphology and corresponding elemental analysis of the mild steel immersed in HCl solution (6 h) in presence and absence of inhibitors were analyzed by FESEM ( Fig. 15) and EDS ( Fig. 16 and Table 7) spectra to understand the severity of corrosion attack on steel the surface. Fig. 15 (a) and (b) displayed mild steel after polishing with SiC paper and after immersion in HCl solution without inhibitor. The polished steel showed an evident smooth surface, while after immersion, the development of a highly rough surface revealed corrosion product formation due to severe corrosion of the surface. The appearance of a reasonably smooth surface after adding N-CDs suggested the effective adsorption of inhibitor and successive barrier film formation resulting in better corrosion protection. The inhibitor adsorbed film was confirmed by EDS elemental analysis with the presence of nitrogen. A decline in oxygen contents compared to the uninhibited surface indicated a decrease in the formation of iron oxides, corrosion products.

The addition of Cu,N-CDs inhibitors with various concentrations of Cu doping depicted a relatively smooth surface compared to the uninhibited one which may be attributed to the adsorption of Cu,N-CDs. The corresponding EDS spectra of Cu,N-CDs inhibited surface confirmed the presence of N and copper. A significant percentage of Cu and a lower proportion of iron and oxygen intimated the existence of Cu film on the steel surface. Furthermore, the Cu contents on the surface increased as we moved from Cu,N-CDs-1 to Cu,N-CDs-3. Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3 having higher Cu+2 ion could produce more homogenous Cu growth by reduction. Consequently, better surface coverage and homogeneity were observed following the order of Cu,N-CDs-3 ˃ Cu,N-CDs-2 ˃ Cu,N-CDs-1. This order can also be asserted from the visual identification of digital photographs. The obtained morphology results are in agreement with electrochemical data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis of inhibitor adsorbed steel surface
ce:section.ce:para: Surface compositions of inhibited mild steel specimens in HCl were examined by XPS technique to get better insights into the corrosion mechanism. The survey spectra of mild steel surface in presence of inhibitors were represented in Fig. 17 (a). The presence of distinguishable N 1 s peak in case inhibited mild steel surface suggested adsorption of inhibitors. Cu 2p peaks in Cu,N-CDs inhibited surface revealed Cu,N-CDs inhibitor adsorption on mild steel. Moreover, the relative intensity of Cu 2p peaks in Cu,N-CDs adsorbed steel is noted to increase with increasing Cu doping concentration, that is, as we move from Cu,N-CDs-1 to Cu,N-CDs-3.

The high resolution elements scan of C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s in N-CDs and C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and Cu 2p in Cu-,N-CDs-2 were depicted in Fig. 17 (b)-(h). The C 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks assigned to C-C/CC of contaminant hydrocarbon as well as contributions from doped CDs. At the same time, C-O/C-N and CO originated from N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs [57]. The O 1 s spectrum yielded three peaks with lower binding energy attributed to O2- in Fe2O3. The peaks at 531.3 and 531.1 eV in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs, respectively, could be related to OH- in hydrous iron oxide, FeOOH/Fe(OH)3 and C-O bonding in doped CDs while peak appearing at 533.3 eV was due to water adsorption on steel surface [58].

The deconvolution of N 1 s spectra resulted in three peaks. The peaks originating at 399.2 eV in N-CDs and 398.2 eV in Cu,N-CDs-2 could be ascribed contributions from unprotonated nitrogen atom of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs-2 inhibitors [59]. The peaks appearing at higher binding energy end, 400.6 eV in N-CDs and 401.3 eV in Cu,N-CDs-2 were linked to protonated nitrogen in acid medium [24,58]. The peak positions at 399.6 eV (N-CDs) and 400.1 eV (Cu,N-CDs-2) may be attributed to metal coordination with the nitrogen atom of doped CDs that is N-Fe bonding [40,60]. Thus, chemical and physical adsorption of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs inhibitors on the mild steel surface could be established. The N-Fe complexation could be explained by donor-acceptor interaction, such as sharing of lone pair of electron on N atom of CDs with the vacant d-orbital of Fe to form a coordinate covalent bond. Moreover, physical adsorption of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs could take place through electrostatic interaction of protonated nitrogen with the negatively charged steel surface.

Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 17 (h)) of Cu,N-CDs-2 inhibited steel surface, displayed spin-orbit splitting of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2. The deconvoluted peaks at 932.6 and 952.4 eV could be attributed to Cu(0). Besides, two peaks located at 934.4 and 954.7 eV may be assigned to Cu(II), which was confirmed by the appearance of two characteristic satellite peaks [61]. This result indicated adsorption of Cu,N-CDs and growth of metallic Cu on mild steel surface. The Cu growth on steel surface was also supported by EDX analysis and visual identification digital photograph Fig. 9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD pattern of mild steel surface in presence and absence inhibitor
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of mild steel surface immersed in the presence and absence of inhibitor was illustrated in Fig. 18. The uninhibited and N-CDs inhibited mild steel showed two peaks at 44.6º and 65.0º, corresponding to the lattice plane of (110) and (200) of pure iron [62]. Besides, Cu,N-CDs-2 inhibited steel exhibited additional peaks of metallic copper at 43.3º and 50.4º, suggesting the presence of Cu(0) on mild steel surface [63]. Thus it was confirmed that weakly coordinated Cu ions in Cu,N-CDs get reduced on mild steel substrate as Cu(0), producing a Cu film.

The surface roughness of mild steel surface after 6 h immersion in the presence and absence of inhibitors (500 ppm) was investigated by the AFM technique. Fig. 19 displayed surface profiles in 3D with average roughness parameter (Ra). Blank mild steel surface revealed highest height profiles with Ra of 79.7 nm, indicating severe corrosion. The Ra decreased in presence of different inhibitors and followed the order Cu,N-CDs-1(40.1 nm) ˂ Cu,N-CDs-2 (28.3 nm) ˂ Cu,N-CDs-3 (26.6) ˂ N-CDs (13 nm). The lower surface roughness in inhibitors suggested decrease in corrosion. The higher roughness in Cu,N-CDs inhibitors compared to N-CDs was attributed to Cu growth on the steel surface. Cu,N-CDs-1 displayed higher surface roughness due to the formation of non-uniform Cu film. However, as the Cu doping concentration increased in Cu,N-CDs that is Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3, the film uniformity increased and hence lower roughness was achieved. The observed relative surface roughness in presence and absence of inhibitors was in agreement with FESEM surface morphology investigation.

The corrosion protection mechanism of studied N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs inhibitors on mild steel in HCl can be deduced from the above characterization results. N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs doped with graphitic, pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen centres with multiple surface functional groups such as -OH, -CO, -COOH and -NH2 can adsorb on the steel surface via physical and chemical adsorption. The physicochemical adsorption of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs was confirmed by a combination of electrochemical analysis, adsorption isotherm, surface morphology and XPS analysis. In general, the surface of mild steel becomes positively charged when immersed in hydrochloric acid solution. As a result, chloride ions readily adsorb onto the positively charged steel surface. It is also quite obvious that N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs could easily be protonated in HCl solution via nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups. Thus, physical adsorption of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs could occur through electrostatic interaction of protonated N atom and other positively charged surface functionalities with negatively charged steel surface generated by preadsorbed chloride ions. Further, unprotonated nitrogen and oxygen atoms in N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs with lone pair of electrons can develop a coordinate bond with vacant d-orbitals of the Fe atom, as evidenced by XPS spectra (N-Fe bond) of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs inhibited steel surface. Reverse electron sharing could also be possible with nonbonded electrons of Fe being donated to empty orbitals of N and O atoms. Therefore, successful physicochemical adsorption of N-CDs can generate a protective film restricting active iron dissolution.

In addition to the adsorbed inhibitor film, Cu ions in adsorbed Cu,N-CDs may reduce to metallic Cu on steel substrate, developing a homogenous Cu film. EDS, XPS and XRD analysis proved the growth of Cu film on the steel surface. The electroless like Cu growth was attributable to the difference in standard reduction potential between Cu (+0.34 V) and Fe (−0.441 V). Besides, because Cu has a higher positive reduction potential than H2 (0 V), the growth of a Cu film could effectively block the cathodic H+ reduction. It may also be noted that effective inhibitor adsorption and compact film formation prevented unwanted anodic oxidation of Fe, i.e. displacement reaction with Cu. Therefore, enhanced corrosion inhibition properties of Cu,N-CDs were ascribed to the combination of adsorbed inhibitor film and growth of copper barrier layer. Fig. 20 illustrates the schematic inhibition mechanism of mild steel corrosion in presence of Cu,N-CDs inhibitor.

In the present study, N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs (Cu,N-CDs-1, Cu,N-CDs-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3) were synthesized and their corrosion inhibition properties for mild steel in 15% HCl solution were thoroughly examined. The successful synthesis of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs was confirmed by FTIR, XRD, TEM, EDS and XPS techniques. The electrochemical analysis validated improved corrosion inhibition performances for all synthesized carbon dots. The inhibition efficiencies were increased when inhibitors concentrations were increased. Further, inhibition efficiencies of Cu,N-CDs were higher than N-CDs. Cu,N-CD-2 and Cu,N-CDs-3 exhibited excellent inhibition efficiency of greater than 99.5% at all studied temperatures as observed in gravimetric measurements. The protective mechanism was associated with physicochemical adsorption and subsequent inhibitor film formation. Surface analysis of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs inhibited steel surfaces confirmed adsorption and the development of inhibitor films. The higher corrosion resistance of Cu,N-CDs was attributed to an electroless like Cu deposition mechanism on the mild steel surface. Moreover, Cu,N-CDs exhibited a predominant cathodic inhibition mechanism by preventing hydrogen evolution reactions. Therefore, it may be concluded that suitable metal ions or atoms doping into CDs could enhance the corrosion protection behaviour of various metals and alloys, which can pave the way for a new area of exploration.

Subash Padhan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Tapan Kumar Rout: Supervision, Project administration, Visualization. Udayabhanu G. Nair: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.
